sentences.text: [TONE]
My name is Thomas Toivi Blatt.
Toivi is my Yiddish name from Poland.
I was born a typical Jewish shtetl, about 3,600 Jews and maybe 200 Christians.
In-- this-- it was town called Izbica, district Krasnystaw, and Lublin.
In Poland.
My father was an invalid.
He was so-called-- he was a Pilsudchik.

He was a volunteer in Pilsudski's army, fighting for the independence of Poland, and because owit-- of it-- he did get a special concession to run a liquor store, which was reserved for special people fighting for independence of Poland.
So we had a steady income, which was unusual in such a little shtetl, and he made a decent living.
My mother, when she was single, she was a teacher in Lublin, and later when the marriage, she moved to Izbica.
I had a little brother four years younger than me, born in 1934.
We-- I finished the public school in Poland until the sixth grade, because the war started later.
My parents were not Orthodox.
My father didn't go, actually, to temple.
Only Yom Kippur, he used to go.
My mother had never seen him-- seen him go to the temple.
So our life was-- in the beginning, I still remember, we used to have holidays.
You know, Hanukkah, Yom Kippur-- but not real Orthodox way.
My relatives is very Orthodox.
My grandmother, she never went to our home, because she was afraid she would be contaminated with treif-- non-kosher stuff.
But I was taken for the holidays to their homes, my grandmothers and my grandfather, which were conducted in strict Orthodox way.
And this was the life in our town.
In our town, actually, Jews kept separate.
So we went-- some were hostile to us, and some were neutral.
But friendly, no.
I mean, friendly like friends coming to each other's house?
No.
I didn't feel it coming, although I remember when my father read the newspaper and was talking about Hitler, about anti-Semitism.
But to me, actually, the-- the war, it started unexpectedly.
I was born in 1927.
I was 12 years when the war started.
So-- but a couple days before the war started, I remember my father was talking about going to the army.
And I was very upset and very scared, I still remember.
I remember people going to the trains.
And I was afraid my father will leave me.
But he never left.
And a couple days later, the bombs start falling on Izbica.
I don't know [INAUDIBLE] Izbica.
But Izbica was a very important factory producing material for bunkers, for military installations.
So probably they were trying to bomb the factories.
I remember bombs falling on the city.
It didn't hurt anybody.
And a couple weeks later, the Germans marched in.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And when they did come, I wasn't afraid.
I wasn't being afraid because the anti-Semitic excesses I read in the paper.
But when we were there, I was surprised that I could understand the language.
It sound to me like Yiddish.
And some kids in my age, they're doing business with them, selling them cigarettes.
So I was relaxed.
But soon after, Gestapo outfit arrived.
They set up their quarters in the mayor's office.
And at that point, terrible things happened.
There were two Gestapo men-- you know, Gestapo, the Secret Service.
One was Engels, and another was named Klem, Ludwig Klem.
And they both, ever they went out like they were hunting Jews.
Simple hunting, like animals.
And they were killing them in the streets.
Sometimes they grabbed five, six people, took them to the cemetery, and killed them there.
And we understood that the Jews had no rights, without any protection.
Izbica was not a ghetto as we imagine a ghetto-- Warsaw, [?
Krakow. ?] Because the whole town was Jewish.
Only the burgermeister was a Pole.
The chief of police was a Pole, chief of the post office.
So there was no need to, to put a section of the town as a ghetto.
The whole, the whole town was regarded as a ghetto.
We had no barbed wires.
But we weren't allowed to go outside the town.
If you were caught outside, you was immediately killed.
Polish police, of course, did help, working with the Nazis in this regard, checking the Jews.
Nevertheless, there was no hunger in our place, because we were able still to mingle with the Poles, go out to the villages, you know, and buy some food.
We weren't allowed, so we were risking with our lives.
Some were shot.
But we did know our ways.
And not too many people were killed because of smuggling food.
This was going on for a while.

Of course, the Nazis once in a while went in the-- I will call it ghetto-- went in where the Jews used to live, and grabbed 100 people, 200, took them to some special project, like building an airstrip or road.
And after the project was finished-- took a month or five weeks-- they let them back to the ghetto.
There was some beating going on at this camp, but nevertheless, people did return.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Everybody was obligated to go to the working camps in turn.
But the rich people were able to pay money to the poor Jews, which were glad to go.
But as the war progressed, the beating-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the cruelty did become more-- was--
--intensified.
And even the poor Jews did refuse to go.
So at that point, the Nazis surrounded the ghetto and picked up people, took them to a camp called Mokre.
They built an airport for-- a military airport.
And they kept the people for about three, four months.
And still some people which had the money were able to buy their way out.
And this lasted until the beginning of 1942.
Visitation was barred.
People were beaten.
They're tortured.
But still, it wasn't that which later did-- did happen.
In 1942, I remember, in about the end of April, our town was surrounded by the Nazis.
And the Nazis picked up everybody on the street.
There was a child or an old man, old woman-- they grabbed them.
They took them to the train station, loaded in the trains.
The train left, and that was all.
We waited a day, two, three, for some news from the people.
Up till now, if the Nazis took some people, after a couple days, there were some letters smuggled out.
Either we're here or there.
Please send us food.
But this time, like a stone in the water, it was quiet.
And we realized something strange happened.
Because when up till now the Nazis were looking for young people able to work, this time they grabbed everybody.
As I said, from the beginning-- from April 1942.
Yeah.
Up to that point, well, mostly they were working and let back home to the ghetto.
But this was a strange situation, because they grabbed everybody, even people who were obvious not able to work-- old men.
So it was, from the beginning, suspicious.
On top of it, there was no news.

So then we asked-- I remember my father had a friend, a Pole, and the other people-- asked Christian friends that if something will happen again, some kind of round-up similar to it, they should go after the train and find out where the train is going, especially when we heard in other towns nearby the same thing happened.
It didn't take long-- beginning of May.
Izbica was again surrounded.
They grabbed about 1,500 people, loaded in the train.
The train was waiting [INAUDIBLE] in the station.
And the train moved eastward.
But this time, the Poles which were alert went after the train.
And it didn't take long.
The same day, they returned and told us a strange story-- unbelievable story-- that about 40 miles farther is a place called Belzec.
And they're killing people there, simple like that.
So we realized that [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the Germans are killing Jews.
But, you see, they have done it in a very special way.
It was-- they planned it, probably, this way.
Because in the beginning, they asked the Judenrat-- now, Judenrat was an organization of the elders-- elder, elder-- the elders of the Jews, which in a way cooperated with the Nazis.
It wasn't a voluntary organization.
The Nazis established, these are called Judenrat.
There were about a dozen very well known Jews.
And the orders were first given to the Judenrat, and the Judenrat implemented.
In the beginning, the Judenrat was ordered to deliver 1,000, 2,000 people.
And the Judenrat picked up, of course, some cripples, old people.
But later, when it was known that the people at the destination is dead, and everybody wants to live, they start to hide.
Dig hiding places under the floors and different kinds.
We build a hiding place in the attic of our home.
It so happened that the burgermeister, the mayor of the town, lived-- did live in the same apartment building.
And our apartment was directly connected to the mayor's apartment.
So in the attic, the attic too was connected.
So we-- simple-- cut into the burgermeister's attic, and we made a double wall.
And we were hidden over there.
We entered through our attic to his part, and we camouflaged.
It was a good hiding place.
[SIGHS]
You see, Izbica-- for a researcher, Izbica's very well known.
Because Izbica was a perfect trap.
Izbica is a town built in a valley.
All around were mountains, hills.
So when there was a round-up, it was enough for the [INAUDIBLE] or the Nazis to stay on top of the hills, and they had a perfect view.
There was no way to escape.
And also, in the beginning, even in the-- in 1940 already, many people from West Poland-- from the Kolo district, Kolo, from the Poznan district-- were actually resettled to Izbica.
And still later, transports from Czechoslovakia, from Germany, Berlin, Frankfurt, were also, also resettled to Izbica.
Izbica was-- as I said, researchers know about this-- a collection point from where the Jews were later taken to Sobibor, to the death camps.
So as I said, Izbica was about 3,600 Jews.
And very fast in the middle of 1942, the amount dropped.
But soon transport from Slovakia, from Czechoslovakia, went in.
And again they filled up the capacity-- about 10,000, 15,000 people.
So they didn't know the hiding places because they hadn't been there very long.
Where did they try to hide?
And they were the first victims.
Because they didn't know.
But some which did live in a house-- like in our place, we had Czech Jews.
They have seen the hiding place, so they went in.
But we had trouble with the German Jews.
When there was a round-up, we told them, listen.
The Germans told you to go to the marketplace.
Don't go, because they take you to a killing place.
But they were brought up in the German discipline, and they picked up something from it.
And they said, no.
An order is  an order.
Now what's important, if you talk about history-- the deception.
The Germans played this ethnic, I would say, difference.
We're all Jews.
But some were from Germany and some from Poland.
It was a time, I remember, when they declared that the German Jews are more civilized, and they could live peaceful.
But the Polish Jews must be delivered.
So they organized a German and Czech Jewish police force, and they were grabbing Jews.
And I remember this.
Now, after that, a month later, it was the opposite.
They called the Polish Judenrat, and they told them, listen.
Have you seen what the Czech Jews, they do to you?
You have a chance to get back.
And so it happened.
And I remember when the Czech police, the Jewish police, were loaded by the Polish Jewish police in the train stations, into the wagons.
Yeah.
And I describe it in my book.
So this way, they were playing this game of deception.
And it, it worked for them.
And so in October 1942, my mother called me to the room upstairs and told me, Tom, Toivi-- we talk Yiddish in our town-- you must save yourself.
We know that the last days are coming for us.
We-- there are few Jews left in Izbica, and any day they could take us to the death camp, Sobibor.
A new death camp was built.
So I will give you Christian papers.
I will be-- I have Christian papers, and I will go hide in Warsaw.
Your father will find a place here with a Polish neighbor together with your brother.
And you with your Christian papers will go with a guide to the Hungarian border and cross the border to Hungary, where according to the rumors, the Jews have it a little bit easier.
And this was true.
Because the Hungarian Jews were resettled-- they called resettled-- taken to Auschwitz to be killed-- in 1944.
And this was in 1942.
Ah.
Let me tell you.
So she said, go to Tarnogora.
Tarnogora was not far from Izbica, and over there, go to the Polish-- to the Pole.
He was a secretary of the mayor's office.
And he has for you a birth certificate.
There's a boy who died a few days ago.
And the secretary of this mayor's office-- he didn't cross him off as-- that he died.
He will give you his papers.
And with these papers, when you will come back, we will, we will prepare you for the trip.
You are leaving tonight, 2 o'clock.
This was so suddenly-- this was October.
I-- the action a few days later was the round-up.
It was about in the middle of October, 1942.
So I remember going to the village.
I picked up the paper.
The secretary's name was [POLISH] He didn't see  me, but his wife did give me the paper.
And while returning back, it was a little dark.
I remember a Polish boy was chasing me-- to beat me up, of course.
Or maybe if he would catch me, he would give me to the-- he would deliver to the police.
And I returned.
And when I did come home, there was my neighbor's son.
He was-- his name was Wolf Schneidmesser And we ate supper.
We had, I remember, [NON-ENGLISH] sardines.
And my mother was sewing in, in my pants, sewing in some gold coins.
And some money paper she did give me, which I put behind my belt.
Everything was so suddenly.
And she said, Toivi, you will go now to Mr.
Schneidmesser's house, our neighbor.
His name was Schneidmesser.
And over there, there are about eight Jews.
They have a Polish guide called [NON-ENGLISH] And he will take you to the Hungarian border.
And over there you will meet some [INAUDIBLE] and the border will take you to Hungary.
When you come to the border, you will pay them.
There were rumors that the guide will take you to the border and on the border they will kill you.
But this will only tell you about the desperation.
So I must take a chance.
So I remember I kissed my mother goodbye, and my brother, which I loved very much, and left.
And it was already dark.
It was illegal to go outside.
But I left, hiding close to the wall of the street.
And suddenly I feel an urge to go back to my parents.
So I turn around and did come back-- go back to our home, to our room.
And my Dad did look at me.
He said, Toivi, you shouldn't do that!
It's a bad sign.
He was suspicious, probably.
He was.
Superstitious.
You shouldn't do that.
And he was very upset, I remember.
So I turned around and left again, and went to Schneidmesser's house.
In Schneidmesser's house was a man called Rappaport from Zamocz.
He had an iron store in Zamocz, I remember, selling iron bars.
And there was Rabinowicz a man who-- he wasn't from Izbica.
But some way, he found his way to Izbica.
There was a girl, Langart-- Langart Tuvna-- a pretty girl, maybe 17 years old.
There were a few people.
I can't remember them all.
And we had already the tickets.
Because the guide, [NON-ENGLISH] bought the tickets and delivered to us.
Train tickets.
Now, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the next step was to steal our way to the train-- not to the station.
But in a small town, there's one little station and a little room, and the train-- the end of the train probably someplace in the field.
And this was in Izbica.
So we left this house in the darkness, and stealed our way to the bushes, where we figure, ah, the end of the train will be.
When the train arrived, we just went into the last wagon.
It was dark, because in that time, there was no light.
It was a war.
It was a-- Blackout.
Blackout.
So we went into, into wagons.
I sit with my friend Wolf Schneidmesser in one, one seat, on the same bench.
And the train left.
The train-- it took us about 45 minutes-- when I see people are going from the compartments to the corridor, looking out of the window.
And I felt some funny smell.
And people, the Polish people, start to talk a bit between each other-- Belzec.
They burn Jews.
So suddenly-- and I look out through the window, and I see this fire.
In another side of the railroad tracks, I see a fire coming from behind bushes.
And suddenly I realized that we are passing Belzec, this place where my, my, most of my family-- not the closest one, but uncles, grandfathers-- were gassed over there.
We're passing Belzec.
And I was practically paralyzed.
[SIGHS]
And finally the train stopped over there, because it was a regular station.
And you could feel the smell of burnt flesh.
And finally the train start again.
And we pass Belzec.
Now, again, about half an hour-- a woman comes, a woman comes to me, a Polish woman.
It was, you know, it was-- it wasn't completely dark, because the moon did light.
And through the window, you could see movement.
A woman did come to me-- a peasant woman, because with her shawl and everything else-- and said, mister, I think that the two people over there are Jewish.
Why did come, she did come to me?
I was a young boy.
I still don't know.
But I think because I was better dressed.
My mother made a mistake.
Instead dress me like everybody else, like smugglers-- a lot of smugglers did go smuggle food from villages, and wear simple dress.
My mother dressed me like, you know, you're going for a big trip-- good socks, and this.
And maybe she taught I'm a [INAUDIBLE], an ethnic German or whatever.
And she said, I think they are Jews.
And she left.
And now I was at a point, I didn't know what to do.
I told her, OK, I will take care of it.
And she left.
She went to her seat.
It didn't take long.
This Rabinovitch-- the Jew which I mentioned left with us-- did come to my place and said, Toivi, I need $10.
Because [NON-ENGLISH] the guide, want more money.
Otherwise he's leaving us.
So I told him, all right, we know we're in trouble, because she just point at you that you are Jewish.
There's a woman who recognized you.
And now you come to me.
And I'm in trouble.
We must do something.
So he was thinking, what should we do?
He said, listen.
The next station, we are leaving.
And we will go to the-- change the wagons.
We're going to change the tape now.
Yeah.
